question: Following the OAuth flow, your custom client POSTs `client_credentials` to the auth URL and receives the response above. What does the docs recommend you do with this access token before requesting another one?

options.text: Cache it for the duration in `expires_in` (24 hours) and reuse it until it expires.
Request a fresh token before every gRPC call so the broker always validates current credentials.
Exchange the returned `refresh_token` whenever the broker rejects a request with `401`.
Pass it as the `audience` field of every follow-up request to identify the cluster.

a.text: `expires_in: 86400` means the token is valid for 86400 seconds (24 hours). The docs explicitly tell you to consider a mechanism to cache the token for the duration before requesting a new one.

b.text: Requesting a new token per call wastes the 24-hour lifetime and adds latency; the docs recommend caching.

c.text: The example response only contains `access_token`, `scope`, `expires_in`, and `token_type` — there is no `refresh_token` to exchange.

d.text: `audience` is part of the request payload that identifies the target (e.g. `zeebe.camunda.io`); it is not where the access token is placed.

explanation: The OAuth example shows the access token comes back with `expires_in: 86400` (24 hours) and the docs advise caching it for that duration before requesting a new one, so clients amortise the cost of token acquisition across many gRPC calls.
